description: Anger is another thing choice is not, since acts due to anger are not especially thought objects of choice.

evidence.note: Aristotle says acts due to anger are thought less than any others objects of choice.

description: Appetite is one of the candidates Aristotle rejects as identical with choice.

evidence.note: Aristotle says appetite is not the same as choice and can conflict with it.

description: Book III narrows temperance and self-indulgence to bodily pleasures, especially those of touch.

evidence.note: Aristotle says temperance must be concerned with bodily pleasures.

description: Choice is deliberate desire of what is in our power and shows character more precisely than bare action does.

evidence.note: Aristotle defines choice as deliberate desire of things in our own power.

description: Compulsion describes cases where the moving principle is outside the agent and he contributes nothing.

evidence.note: Aristotle defines the compulsory by an external moving principle to which the agent contributes nothing.

description: Courage is the mean with respect to fear and confidence, especially where noble danger and death are at stake.

evidence.note: Aristotle says courage is the mean with respect to things that inspire confidence or fear.

description: Cowardice is the excess of fear and lack of confidence opposed to courage.

evidence.note: Aristotle describes the coward as exceeding in fear and lacking confidence.

description: Deliberation concerns what can be done by us and works through the means to an assumed end.

evidence.note: Aristotle says deliberation is about means rather than ends and traces how an end is to be achieved.

description: The end is what we wish for, while choice and deliberation address the means toward it.

evidence.note: Aristotle says the end is what we wish for.

source_labels: things that inspire confidence or fear
description: Fear and confidence are the sphere in which Aristotle distinguishes courage, rashness, and cowardice.

evidence.note: Aristotle says courage is a mean with respect to what inspires confidence or fear.

description: Ignorance of the particulars can make an action involuntary when the action later brings pain and repentance.

evidence.note: Aristotle treats ignorance of the circumstances and end of the action as what grounds involuntary action.

description: The involuntary covers actions done under compulsion or owing to the relevant kind of ignorance.

evidence.note: Aristotle opens by saying the involuntary is what takes place under compulsion or owing to ignorance.

description: Means are the doable steps through which an end is pursued and about which choice and deliberation are concerned.

evidence.note: Aristotle says we deliberate not about ends but about means.

description: Opinion differs from choice because it is assessed by truth and falsity rather than by moral quality.

evidence.note: Aristotle distinguishes opinion from choice by its different objects and standards.

description: Rashness is the excess in confidence that imitates courage without standing firm before what is truly terrible.

evidence.note: Aristotle names the excessive type rash and says he is a pretender to courage.

description: Self-indulgence is the culpable excess with respect to bodily pleasures.

evidence.note: Aristotle says excess with regard to pleasures is self-indulgence and is culpable.

description: Temperance is the mean with respect to bodily pleasures and the appetites attached to them.

evidence.note: Aristotle introduces temperance as the mean with regard to pleasures.

description: The voluntary is what has its moving principle in the agent, with awareness of the particular circumstances.

evidence.note: Aristotle defines the voluntary by the moving principle being in the agent together with awareness of the particulars.

description: Wish is directed toward the end rather than the means that bring the end about.

evidence.note: Aristotle explicitly says the end is what we wish for.
